section.title: Oholah and Oholibah
section.sectionRationale: This is Ezekiel's second, even more explicit allegory of two sisters, Oholah, Samaria, and Oholibah, Jerusalem, whose names play on the Hebrew word for tent, marking the city where God's own tent, the temple, truly stood as the more culpable of the two. Their shared history of political and religious 'prostitution,' pursuing alliances with Assyria and Babylon as spiritual adultery against the LORD, is confronted with the same shocking honesty as chapter 16, handled with the same pastoral care: acknowledged plainly, never treated as entertainment, and never separated from the reality of God's own grief and righteous jealousy.
section.thesis: Two sisters, Samaria and Jerusalem, are exposed as having pursued the very political and religious alliances they should have refused, Jerusalem's betrayal graver still because God's own dwelling stood within her walls, and both are judged by the very nations they once desired, a devastating picture of covenant unfaithfulness that finds its full and final answer only in Christ, the faithful Bridegroom who does not abandon His unfaithful people but cleanses and presents His church spotless.
section.frameNote: Six movements: two sisters introduced, and the elder, Oholah, falls to her lovers the Assyrians; the younger, Oholibah, proves even more depraved, adding Babylon to her pursuits; judgment announced as her own former lovers turn destroyers; a shared cup of judgment drained down to its dregs; shared abominations including child sacrifice and a defiled sanctuary; and the sentence finally executed, ending this indecency as a warning to all.

units.label: Two sisters, and the elder's fall to Assyria

verses.text: Again the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, there were two women, daughters of the same mother,
and they played in Egypt, prostituting themselves from their youth. Their breasts were fondled there, and their virgin bosoms caressed.

The older was named {{oholah_oholibah|Oholah}}, and her sister was named {{oholah_oholibah|Oholibah}}. They became Mine and gave birth to sons and daughters. As for their identities, Oholah is Samaria, and Oholibah is Jerusalem.
Oholah {{zanah|prostituted}} herself while she was still Mine. She lusted after her lovers, the Assyrians, warriors
clothed in blue, governors and commanders, all desirable young men, horsemen mounted on steeds.
She offered sexual favors to all the elite of Assyria. She defiled herself with all the idols of those for whom she lusted.
She did not give up the prostitution she began in Egypt, when men slept with her in her youth, caressed her virgin bosom, and poured out their lust upon her.
Therefore I delivered her into the hands of her lovers, the Assyrians for whom she lusted.
They exposed her nakedness, seized her sons and daughters, and put her to the sword. Thus she became a byword among women, and they executed judgment against her.
Her sister Oholibah saw this, yet in her lust and prostitution she was more depraved than her sister.
She too lusted after the Assyrians, governors and commanders, warriors dressed in splendor, horsemen riding on steeds, all desirable young men.
And I saw that she too had defiled herself; both of them had taken the same path.
But Oholibah carried her prostitution even further. She saw the men portrayed on the wall, images of the Chaldeans, engraved in vermilion,
wearing belts on their waists and flowing turbans on their heads; all of them looked like officers of the Babylonians in Chaldea, the land of their birth.
At the sight of them, she lusted for them and sent messengers to them in Chaldea.
Then the Babylonians came to her, to the bed of love, and in their lust they defiled her. But after she had been defiled by them, she turned away in disgust.
When Oholibah openly prostituted herself and exposed her nakedness, I turned away from her in disgust, just as I had turned away from her sister.
Yet she multiplied her promiscuity, remembering the days of her youth, when she had prostituted herself in the land of Egypt
and lusted after their lovers, whose genitals were like those of donkeys and whose emission was like that of stallions.
So you revisited the indecency of your youth, when the Egyptians caressed your bosom and pressed your young breasts.

units.label: Judgment: her own lovers turn against her
units.note: the very nations she pursued become her destroyers

verses.text: Therefore, Oholibah, this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘I will incite your lovers against you, those from whom you turned away in disgust. And I will bring them against you from every side,

the Babylonians and all the Chaldeans, the men of Pekod, Shoa, and Koa, and all the Assyrians with them, all desirable young men, governors and commanders, officers and men of renown, mounted on horses.

They will come against you with a host of peoples, with weapons, chariots, and wagons. They will array themselves against you on every side with buckler and shield and helmet. I will delegate judgment to them, and they will punish you according to their own standards.

And I will set My {{qinah|jealous rage}} against you, and they will deal with you in fury. They will cut off your noses and ears, and your survivors will fall by the sword. They will seize your sons and daughters, and your remnant will be consumed by fire.
They will strip off your clothes and take your fine jewelry.
So I will put an end to your indecency and prostitution, which began in the land of Egypt, and you will not lift your eyes to them or remember Egypt anymore.’
For this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Surely I will deliver you into the hands of those you hate, from whom you turned away in disgust.
They will treat you with hatred, take all for which you have worked, and leave you naked and bare, so that the shame of your prostitution will be exposed. Your indecency and promiscuity
have brought these things upon you, because you have prostituted yourself with the nations and defiled yourself with their idols.

units.label: Her sister's cup, drained to the dregs

verses.text: Because you have followed the path of your sister, I will put her cup into your hand.’
This is what the Lord GOD says: ‘You will drink your sister's {{kos|cup}}, a cup deep and wide. It will bring scorn and derision, for it holds so much.
You will be filled with drunkenness and grief, with a cup of devastation and desolation, the cup of your sister Samaria.
You will drink it and drain it; you will dash it to pieces, and tear your breasts. For I have spoken,’ declares the Lord GOD.
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Because you have forgotten Me and have cast Me behind your back, you must bear the consequences of your indecency and prostitution.’”

units.label: Shared abominations: child sacrifice and a defiled sanctuary
units.note: the same day they slaughtered children, they entered My sanctuary

verses.text: Then the LORD said to me: “Son of man, will you pass judgment against Oholah and Oholibah? Then declare to them their abominations.

For they have committed adultery, and blood is on their hands. They have committed adultery with their idols. They have even sacrificed their children, whom they bore to Me, in the fire as food for their idols.
They have also done this to Me: On that very same day, they defiled My sanctuary and profaned My Sabbaths.
On the very day they slaughtered their children for their idols, they entered My sanctuary to profane it. Yes, they did this inside My house.
Furthermore, you sisters sent messengers for men who came from afar; and behold, when they arrived, you bathed for them, painted your eyes, and adorned yourself with jewelry.
You sat on a couch of luxury with a table spread before it, on which you had set My incense and My oil,
accompanied by the sound of a carefree crowd. Drunkards were brought in from the desert along with men from the rabble, who put bracelets on your wrists and beautiful crowns on your head.

units.label: The sentence executed; indecency ended in the land
units.note: a warning to every woman not to imitate this behavior

verses.text: Then I said of her who had grown old in adulteries: ‘Now let them use her as a prostitute, for that is all she is!’
And they slept with her as with a prostitute; they slept with Oholah and Oholibah, those lewd women.
But righteous men will sentence them to the punishment of those who commit adultery and bloodshed, because they are adulteresses with blood on their hands.
This is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Bring a mob against them and consign them to terror and plunder.
The mob will stone them and cut them down with their swords. They will kill their sons and daughters and burn down their houses.
So I will put an end to indecency in the land, and all the women will be admonished not to imitate your behavior.
They will repay you for your indecency, and you will bear the consequences of your sins of idolatry. Then you will know that I am the Lord GOD.’”

oholah_oholibah.gloss: Her tent / My tent is in her. Samaria's name means simply 'her own tent,' a shrine of her own making, while Jerusalem's name, 'my tent is in her,' marks her as the city where God's true tent, the temple, actually stood, a wordplay explaining why Jerusalem's betrayal, described as surpassing her sister's, verse 11, is judged so much more severely: greater privilege bore greater responsibility.

zanah.gloss: To act as a prostitute, to be sexually unfaithful. Used throughout this chapter, and its sister-chapter 16, as the primary metaphor for covenant idolatry, Israel and Judah's pursuit of foreign gods and foreign alliances pictured as marital betrayal of the LORD.

qinah.gloss: Jealousy or jealous rage, the passionate response of a betrayed covenant partner rather than cold cruelty. God's qinah against Oholibah reflects the same relational anguish behind the marriage language of the whole chapter, wounded love responding to betrayal, not indifferent wrath.

kos.gloss: Cup, here specifically a cup of divine wrath, deep and wide, that Jerusalem must drink after her sister Samaria. This image of judgment's inescapable, shared consequence recurs throughout the prophets, Isaiah 51:17, Jeremiah 25:15-29, and reappears in Revelation 14:10.

basic.context: This is Ezekiel's second, even more graphic allegory of covenant unfaithfulness, picturing Samaria and Jerusalem as two sisters, Oholah and Oholibah, whose very names, playing on the Hebrew word for tent, mark Jerusalem's betrayal as the graver of the two, since God's own tent, the temple, actually stood within her. Both sisters pursue the political and military powers of their day, Assyria and then Babylon, as illicit lovers, and both are ultimately destroyed by the very powers they once desired, ending in a stark warning against every future repetition of this behavior.
basic.hermeneutics: As with chapter 16, this is deliberately shocking allegory, using the ancient Near Eastern language of marital infidelity to expose the seriousness of political and religious alliance-seeking as spiritual adultery against the LORD. Its graphic imagery should be read with the same pastoral honesty as its sister chapter, acknowledged plainly for what it communicates about sin's true nature, never sanitized into vagueness nor lingered over for its own sake.
basic.originalLanguages: Oholah, 'her own tent,' and Oholibah, 'my tent is in her,' name Samaria and Jerusalem respectively, the wordplay itself explaining why Jerusalem's zanah, prostitution, described as exceeding her sister's, is judged more severely: greater nearness to God's true dwelling meant greater accountability. Judgment falls as God's own qinah, jealous rage, and as a shared kos, cup, of wrath both sisters must drain.
basic.typology: Jerusalem's judgment for defiling the very sanctuary where God's tent stood contrasts sharply with the New Testament's picture of the church as God's true and lasting temple, and Jerusalem's serial unfaithfulness as a bride, despite unique privilege, is answered by Christ's unwavering faithfulness to His own bride, the church, whom He cleanses and presents to Himself without spot or blemish.
basic.christ: Where Oholibah, privileged above her sister by God's own dwelling among her, proves even more unfaithful, the New Testament presents Christ as the Bridegroom who, though His bride has every advantage of nearness to Him, remains faithful even when she does not, loving the church and giving Himself up for her so that He might sanctify her and present her to Himself in splendor, Ephesians 5:25-27, succeeding perfectly where Jerusalem, and every human heart, repeatedly fails.
basic.god: God's anger in this chapter is the anger of betrayed, covenant love, not cold cruelty; He describes His own reaction in strikingly personal terms, disgust, jealousy, grief, showing that idolatry wounds Him relationally, not merely violates an abstract rule. Yet even this severe chapter's warning, that such indecency will finally end, carries within it God's ultimate purpose that His people would live in a way that reflects, rather than betrays, His holiness.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 3:6-11 similarly compares Israel and Judah as two unfaithful sisters, with Judah judged more severely for having seen her sister's judgment and remaining unrepentant still. Hosea 1-3 tells a parallel story of covenant infidelity and pursuing love. Ephesians 5:25-27 describes Christ's sanctifying, cleansing love for His bride, the church, in deliberate contrast to this chapter's picture of betrayal. Revelation 17-19 contrasts the great prostitute Babylon with the pure bride, the New Jerusalem, adorned for her husband.
basic.oneStory: This is the story of two sisters whose privileged nearness to God became the very measure of their unfaithfulness, judged at last by the very powers they had pursued as lovers. Yet the God who describes His own grief and jealousy so personally in this chapter is the same God who, in Christ, remains faithful to an unfaithful bride, cleansing and presenting her to Himself in splendor, succeeding everywhere Oholah and Oholibah failed.

context.literary: A second, even more graphic sister-allegory paralleling chapter 16, structured around the fates of Oholah and Oholibah in turn, moving from their shared origins in Egypt, through escalating unfaithfulness with Assyria and then Babylon, to judgment delivered by their own former lovers, and closing with a formal legal sentence and warning.
context.historical: Reflects Israel and Judah's actual historical foreign policy across the eighth through sixth centuries BC, the northern kingdom's alliances and eventual destruction by Assyria in 722 BC, and Judah's later shifting alliances with Assyria, then Egypt, then Babylon, culminating in Jerusalem's own destruction in 586 BC, all read here as spiritual adultery rather than mere statecraft.
context.canonical: Directly parallels and intensifies Ezekiel 16's allegory, shares its central metaphor with Jeremiah 3:6-11's comparison of the two sisters, and, like chapter 16, feeds into Revelation's later contrast between the unfaithful prostitute Babylon and the faithful bride, the New Jerusalem, Revelation 17-19, 21.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose, through the shock of an extended marital-infidelity allegory, the full seriousness of Israel and Judah's political and religious alliance-seeking as covenant betrayal against the LORD, showing that greater privilege, Jerusalem's possession of God's own dwelling, brings greater responsibility and graver judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a specific historical and theological indictment of Samaria and Jerusalem's covenant unfaithfulness, told through deliberately shocking allegory; it is not a manual for marriage, nor license to use explicit imagery casually or gratuitously in teaching. Its enduring truth is the seriousness of idolatry as covenant betrayal and the reality that privilege increases, rather than excuses, accountability.

debates.question: How should preachers and teachers responsibly handle this chapter's extremely graphic sexual imagery, arguably even more explicit than chapter 16's?

views.label: Read plainly but with pastoral restraint, as in chapter 16
views.case: The same principle applies here as in chapter 16: the shock is intentional and theologically necessary, so the substance should be acknowledged honestly rather than skipped, while being handled with care for survivors of abuse and constant attention to the text's ultimate warning against unfaithfulness.

views.label: Summarize the allegory's meaning without reading every explicit verse aloud
views.case: Many teachers, especially in mixed or vulnerable settings, choose to summarize the narrative's escalating unfaithfulness and its consequences without dwelling on the most graphic individual verses, judging the theological point can be made faithfully without extended focus on the most explicit language.

debates.bearing: Both approaches agree the imagery is deliberate and meaningful, not to be censored out of existence or treated as sensational, and that the passage's purpose is always to expose the seriousness of covenant unfaithfulness, never to titillate or shame vulnerable hearers.

study.originalLanguages: Oholah, 'her own tent,' and Oholibah, 'my tent is in her,' name Samaria and Jerusalem respectively, the wordplay itself explaining why Jerusalem's zanah, prostitution, described as exceeding her sister's, is judged more severely: greater nearness to God's true dwelling meant greater accountability. Judgment falls as God's own qinah, jealous rage, and as a shared kos, cup, of wrath both sisters must drain to the dregs.

typology.type: Jerusalem's defiling of God's sanctuary while still called by His name, Ezekiel 23:38-39
typology.fulfillment: Contrasted with the New Testament's picture of the church as God's true and lasting temple, indwelt and kept holy by His own Spirit, 1 Corinthians 3:16-17.

typology.type: Two sisters, both unfaithful despite covenant privilege
typology.fulfillment: Answered by Christ's unwavering faithfulness to His bride, the church, whom He loves, cleanses, and presents to Himself without spot or wrinkle, Ephesians 5:25-27.

study.christ: Where Oholibah, privileged above her sister by God's own dwelling among her, proves even more unfaithful, the New Testament presents Christ as the Bridegroom who, though His bride has every advantage of nearness to Him, remains faithful even when she does not, loving the church and giving Himself up for her so that He might sanctify her and present her to Himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle, Ephesians 5:25-27, succeeding perfectly where Jerusalem, and every human heart, repeatedly fails.
study.god: God's anger in this chapter is the anger of betrayed, covenant love, not cold cruelty; He describes His own reaction in strikingly personal terms, disgust, jealousy, grief, showing that idolatry wounds Him relationally, not merely violates an abstract rule. Yet even this severe chapter's warning, that such indecency will finally end, carries within it God's ultimate purpose that His people would live in a way that reflects, rather than betrays, His holiness.

crossReferences.note: Similarly compares Israel and Judah as two unfaithful sisters, with Judah judged more severely for remaining unrepentant even after seeing her sister's judgment.
Tells a parallel story of covenant infidelity met with persistent, pursuing divine love.
Describes Christ's sanctifying, cleansing love for His bride, the church, in deliberate contrast to this chapter's picture of betrayal.
Contrasts the great prostitute Babylon with the pure bride, the New Jerusalem, adorned for her husband.

study.oneStory: This is the story of two sisters whose privileged nearness to God became the very measure of their unfaithfulness, judged at last by the very powers they had pursued as lovers. Yet the God who describes His own grief and jealousy so personally in this chapter is the same God who, in Christ, remains faithful to an unfaithful bride, cleansing and presenting her to Himself in splendor, succeeding everywhere Oholah and Oholibah failed.

sermon.title: No Fidelity Without Remembering (Ezek. 16; 23)
sermon.why: Carson's message works through both Ezekiel 16 and 23 together, directly addressing this chapter's picture of Oholah and Oholibah as a second, intensified allegory of Israel and Judah's covenant unfaithfulness, and lands on the same call to remember God's covenant faithfulness despite repeated betrayal that this study follows.

quiz.q: What do the names Oholah and Oholibah represent, and what wordplay do they contain?

options.text: Samaria and Jerusalem, with names playing on the Hebrew word for 'tent,' Jerusalem's name marking her as the city where God's true tent, the temple, stood.
options.note: Verse 4 identifies the sisters directly, and the names' wordplay on 'tent' underlies why Jerusalem's guilt is judged as greater.

options.text: Two entirely fictional cities with no historical referent.
options.note: Verse 4 explicitly identifies them as Samaria and Jerusalem, real historical cities.

options.text: Egypt and Assyria, the two foreign powers named in the allegory.
options.note: Egypt and Assyria are the lovers pursued in the allegory, not the two sisters themselves.

options.text: Two unrelated tribes with no shared origin.
options.note: Verse 2 specifically calls them 'daughters of the same mother,' emphasizing their shared origin, not an unrelated status.

quiz.q: According to verse 11, how does Oholibah's unfaithfulness compare to her sister's?

options.text: She was less depraved than her sister, having learned from Oholah's example.
options.note: Verse 11 states the opposite: she was more depraved than her sister, not less.

options.text: She was more depraved in her lust and prostitution than her sister.
options.note: Verse 11 states this directly: 'in her lust and prostitution she was more depraved than her sister.'

options.text: She was equally unfaithful, with no difference noted between the sisters.
options.note: The text specifically ranks Oholibah's guilt as exceeding her sister's, not merely equal.

options.text: She remained faithful while only her sister was unfaithful.
options.note: Verse 11 describes Oholibah's own unfaithfulness directly, not faithfulness.

quiz.q: Who ultimately executes judgment against Oholibah, according to verses 22-24?

options.text: A coalition of angels sent directly from heaven.
options.note: The judgment is carried out by human armies, the very nations she once pursued as lovers, not angelic beings.

options.text: Egypt alone, acting in isolation from other nations.
options.note: The judgment involves Babylonians, Chaldeans, and Assyrians together, not Egypt acting alone.

options.text: The very lovers she once pursued and then turned from in disgust, incited by God against her.
options.note: Verses 22-24 describe God inciting her former lovers, Babylonians, Chaldeans, and Assyrians, against her.

options.text: No one; the judgment described never actually takes place in the text.
options.note: The judgment is described as certain and detailed, not left unfulfilled or hypothetical.

quiz.q: What shared abomination involving children is named in verses 37-39?

options.text: Neglecting to educate their children in the law.
options.note: The abomination named is far graver, the sacrifice of children in fire, not educational neglect.

options.text: Sacrificing their children in the fire to idols, even entering God's sanctuary to profane it on the same day.
options.note: Verses 37-39 state this explicitly, naming both the child sacrifice and the same-day defiling of the sanctuary.

options.text: Sending their children away as required tribute to foreign powers.
options.note: The text describes child sacrifice to idols specifically, not the payment of children as political tribute.

options.text: Refusing to have children as an act of protest.
options.note: The passage describes the opposite issue, bearing children to God and then sacrificing them, not childlessness.

quiz.q: What is the stated purpose of the sentence carried out in the chapter's closing verses, 43-49?

options.text: To reward the sisters for their political alliances.
options.note: The passage describes judgment and punishment, not reward, for the sisters' behavior.

options.text: To put an end to indecency in the land and warn all women not to imitate this behavior.
options.note: Verse 48 states this purpose directly: 'I will put an end to indecency in the land, and all the women will be admonished not to imitate your behavior.'

options.text: To restore both sisters immediately to their former royal privileges.
options.note: The passage describes judgment being carried out, not an immediate restoration of privilege.

options.text: To have no lasting effect on future behavior at all.
options.note: The stated purpose is explicitly corrective and preventive, meant to end this pattern, not to be inconsequential.

questions: Jerusalem's greater privilege, God's own dwelling within her, became the measure of her greater guilt when she was unfaithful. Where has God given you particular closeness or blessing that carries with it a greater responsibility you may be neglecting?

Both sisters pursued powerful political alliances as a substitute for trusting God alone. What 'alliances,' whether relationships, achievements, or security measures, are you tempted to lean on instead of trusting God?
The very nations Oholibah once desired became the instruments of her judgment. Have you ever seen something you pursued as a source of security or pleasure turn out to bring you harm instead?

This chapter names God's own grief, disgust, and jealousy at betrayal in strikingly personal terms. How does it change your view of sin to see it primarily as a wound to a relationship with God rather than merely breaking an abstract rule?

Where Jerusalem repeatedly failed to remain faithful despite every privilege, Christ remains faithful to His unfaithful people and cleanses them fully. Where do you need to rest in Christ's faithfulness rather than your own record of faithfulness to Him?

translationNotes.note: The names Oholah and Oholibah are both built from the Hebrew word 'ohel,' tent, a deliberate wordplay likely alluding to the tabernacle and later temple, marking Jerusalem's name as claiming God's own tent uniquely 'in her,' which the allegory uses to explain her graver guilt.

'Pekod, Shoa, and Koa' are place names associated with Babylonian provinces or peoples, mentioned also in Jeremiah 50:21; their inclusion underscores the comprehensive, coalition nature of the judgment brought against Oholibah.

The image of drinking a 'cup' of judgment is a common prophetic metaphor, Isaiah 51:17, Jeremiah 25:15-29, picturing divine wrath as an intoxicating drink whose effects, staggering, shame, and collapse, are unavoidable once poured out.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 23:1-49, grouped into six thematic units given the passage's length, 49 verses. No em or en dashes were present in the source verses. Sermon link verified via live web fetch this session, The Gospel Coalition's Carson Center podcast, whose title explicitly covers both Ezekiel 16 and 23; this material contains graphic covenant-infidelity imagery from the source text itself and has been annotated with pastoral care rather than omitted, per the assignment's instruction to handle it honestly and never gratuitously, matching the approach used for chapter 16.
